title: Lecture 3: New Product Development Part 2 — Concept Screening and Testing

cards.definition: Evaluating competitive products against customer needs and objective metrics to set realistic target specification values.
A matrix tool for rapid, qualitative comparison of multiple product concepts against a reference concept using a +/0/– scale. Net score = Plusses minus Minuses.
A weighted matrix tool that rates concepts on a 1–5 numerical scale across importance-weighted criteria, producing a total weighted score for ranking and selection.

The phase in which selected product concepts are validated against customer and strategic criteria to eliminate concepts that are technically unfeasible, strategically misaligned, or lacking market fit.
A formally structured, product-attribute expression of what the customer requires, written in positive "what not how" language without solution prescription or obligatory phrasing.
A strategy of committing to final specifications early in development, enabling downstream tasks to begin with firm inputs and facilitating cost and performance optimisation.
The best achievable value for a product specification metric, representing the target if no constraints existed; contrasted with the Marginal Value.
A customer insight framework asserting that people "hire" products to accomplish a specific job in a specific situation: "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [desired outcome]."
A strategy of committing to final specifications as late as possible in development, allowing better alignment with evolving customer needs and competitive dynamics.
The minimum acceptable value for a product specification metric — the threshold below which the product fails to satisfy the need.
The measurable property used to operationalise a customer need as a product specification; combined with a value and units to form a complete specification.

A formal document defining a product's description, benefit proposition, key business goals, primary and secondary markets, constraints, assumptions, and stakeholders; used to scope customer needs analysis.
A framework representing a product concept as the intersection of at least two of three inputs — customer Need/Benefit, physical Form, and enabling Technology. A new product requires all three.
A verbal or prototype statement describing what will change in a product and how the customer stands to gain or lose; requires at least two of Need, Form, and Technology to be feasible.
The quantified, measurable translation of a customer needs statement into engineering terms; composed of Metric + Value(s) + Units.

The baseline concept used as the comparison standard in Concept Screening and Scoring Matrices; typically the current/existing product; assigned a score of 0 (screening) or 3 (scoring) on all criteria.

The raw, verbatim language in which customers express their needs, frustrations, and desires, gathered through interviews, focus groups, observation, and ethnography; the starting input to customer needs translation.
The product of a criterion's importance weight and a concept's rating on that criterion in the Concept Scoring Matrix; summed across all criteria to produce the Total Score.
